body:
JavaScript Mastery Bible

ES2025, async patterns & core foundations

JavaScript

95 questions — basic to very advanced. Click any question to reveal the answer.

Showing 95 questions
BWhat is JavaScript?+

A high-level, interpreted, dynamically-typed programming language primarily used for web development. It runs in browsers and on servers (Node.js). It's single-threaded but non-blocking via the event loop.

BWhat are the 8 data types in JavaScript?+

Primitives (7): string, number, bigint, boolean, undefined, symbol, null. Non-primitive (1): object (includes arrays, functions, dates, RegExp, etc.).

BWhat is the difference between == and ===?+

== compares after type coercion ('5'==5 is true). === compares value AND type ('5'===5 is false). Always use === to avoid unexpected bugs.

BWhat is the difference between null and undefined?+

undefined: declared but not assigned, missing parameter, missing property. null: intentionally empty, explicitly assigned. typeof null is 'object' (famous bug). null == undefined is true, null === undefined is false.

BWhat are var, let, and const?+

var: function-scoped, hoisted as undefined, can redeclare. let: block-scoped, TDZ, can reassign. const: block-scoped, must initialize, can't reassign (but object contents ARE mutable). Rule: default to const, use let for reassignment, never use var.

BWhat is hoisting?+

JS moves declarations to the top of their scope before execution. var is hoisted as undefined. let/const are hoisted but NOT initialized (TDZ — ReferenceError if accessed early). Function declarations are fully hoisted. Function expressions are NOT.

BWhat are falsy values?+

Eight values: false, 0, -0, 0n, "", null, undefined, NaN. Everything else is truthy — including "0", "false", [], {}.

BWhat is the difference between function declaration and expression?+

Declaration: function foo(){} — fully hoisted, callable before definition. Expression: const foo = function(){} — NOT hoisted, only callable after assignment. Arrow functions are always expressions.

BWhat are arrow functions?+

Shorter syntax ()=>{}. Key differences: no own this (lexical), no arguments object, can't be used as constructors, no prototype. Use for callbacks; use regular functions for methods and constructors.

BWhat is the spread operator?+

... expands iterables. Arrays: [...a,...b] merge, [...arr] copy. Objects: {...o1,...o2} merge. Function args: fn(...args). Rest (collecting): function(...args), const {a,...rest}=obj.

BWhat is destructuring?+

Extract values from arrays/objects into variables. Object: const {name, age=25} = user. Array: const [first,,third] = arr. Nested: const {address:{city}} = user. Rename: const {name:userName} = user.

BWhat is template literal?+

Backtick strings supporting interpolation `Hello ${name}`, multi-line strings, and tagged templates tag`text ${expr}` where tag is a function.

BWhat is the typeof operator?+

Returns type as string: typeof 'hi'→'string', typeof 42→'number', typeof true→'boolean', typeof undefined→'undefined', typeof null→'object' (bug!), typeof {}→'object', typeof []→'object', typeof function(){}→'function'.

BWhat is NaN?+

'Not a Number' — result of invalid math (0/0, parseInt('abc')). typeof NaN is 'number'. NaN !== NaN is true (the only value not equal to itself). Check with Number.isNaN(x) (not global isNaN which coerces).

BWhat is the difference between for...in and for...of?+

for...in: iterates object keys (includes prototype chain — guard with hasOwnProperty). for...of: iterates values of iterables (arrays, strings, Maps, Sets — NOT plain objects). Use Object.entries() + for...of for objects.

BWhat are truthy and falsy values used for?+

Implicit boolean conversion in conditions: if(value), value && doSomething(), value || default, ternary value ? a : b. Common gotcha: 0 and '' are falsy but might be valid values — use ?? for null/undefined only.

BWhat is scope in JavaScript?+

Where variables are accessible. Global: everywhere. Function: inside the function (var). Block: inside {} (let/const). Scope chain: inner functions can access outer scope variables (not vice versa). This creates closures.

BWhat is a callback function?+

A function passed as an argument to another function, to be called later. Examples: setTimeout(callback, 1000), arr.map(callback), btn.addEventListener('click', callback). Callbacks enable async patterns but can lead to 'callback hell' (nested callbacks).

BWhat is the DOM?+

Document Object Model — a tree representation of HTML that JavaScript can manipulate. Methods: getElementById, querySelector, createElement, appendChild, addEventListener. The browser builds the DOM from HTML, then renders it visually.

BWhat is JSON?+

JavaScript Object Notation — a text format for data exchange. JSON.stringify(obj) converts to string. JSON.parse(str) converts back. Limitations: no functions, no undefined, no NaN/Infinity, no circular references, dates become strings.

BWhat is the difference between slice, splice, and split?+

slice(start,end): returns new array/string portion, doesn't mutate. splice(start,deleteCount,...items): modifies original array in-place. split(separator): string method, splits into array. Modern: use toSpliced() for immutable splice.

BWhat are higher-order functions?+

Functions that take functions as arguments or return functions. Examples: map, filter, reduce, forEach, sort, find, some, every. They enable functional programming patterns and method chaining.

BWhat is map, filter, reduce?+

map(fn): transforms each element, returns new array of same length. filter(fn): keeps elements where fn returns true, returns shorter array. reduce(fn, init): accumulates all elements into single value. All return new arrays (don't mutate original).

BWhat is optional chaining (?.) ?+

Short-circuits to undefined if a property is null/undefined: user?.address?.city instead of user && user.address && user.address.city. Works on properties, arrays arr?.[0], and methods fn?.().

BWhat is nullish coalescing (??)?+

?? returns the right side only when left is null or undefined (not for 0 or ''). Compare: 0 || 'default' → 'default' (0 is falsy). 0 ?? 'default' → 0 (0 is not null/undefined). Use ?? when 0 or empty string are valid values.

BWhat is a Promise?+

An object representing eventual completion/failure of an async operation. Three states: pending → fulfilled (resolved) or rejected. Created with new Promise((resolve, reject) => {}). Consumed with .then(), .catch(), .finally(). Enables cleaner async code than callbacks.

BWhat is async/await?+

Syntactic sugar for Promises. async function always returns a Promise. await pauses execution until Promise resolves. Use try/catch for error handling. Makes async code look synchronous and readable.

BWhat is the difference between let and const for objects?+

const prevents reassignment of the variable binding, NOT mutation of the value. const obj = {} → obj.name = 'hi' works (mutation). obj = {} fails (reassignment). To truly freeze: Object.freeze(obj) — but it's shallow.

BWhat are default parameters?+

function greet(name = 'World') {} — default used when argument is undefined (not null). Works with destructuring: function({x = 0, y = 0} = {}) {}. Evaluated left-to-right, later params can reference earlier ones.

BWhat is event bubbling?+

When an event fires on an element, it 'bubbles' up through its ancestors: target → parent → grandparent → document. You can stop it with e.stopPropagation(). The opposite (capturing) goes top-down. Default is bubbling phase.

BWhat is the difference between innerHTML, textContent, and innerText?+

innerHTML: gets/sets HTML markup (parses tags, XSS risk). textContent: gets/sets raw text of all elements (fastest, includes hidden). innerText: gets visible text only (triggers reflow, slower). Prefer textContent for setting text safely.

BWhat is Array.isArray()?+

The reliable way to check if a value is an array. typeof [] returns 'object' (not helpful). [] instanceof Array fails across frames/iframes. Array.isArray([]) → true, Array.isArray({}) → false.

BWhat is a pure function?+

A function that: 1) always returns the same output for the same inputs, and 2) has no side effects (doesn't modify external state). const add = (a,b) => a+b is pure. Math.random() and functions that modify global variables are impure.

BWhat is the rest parameter?+

function sum(...nums) {} collects remaining arguments into an array. Unlike arguments (array-like, not a real array), rest params ARE real arrays. Must be the last parameter. Works in destructuring: const [first, ...rest] = arr.

BWhat is String interpolation?+

Embedding expressions in template literals: `Hello ${name}, you are ${age} years old`. Any valid JS expression works inside ${} — function calls, ternaries, arithmetic. Much cleaner than string concatenation with +.

IExplain closures with a practical example.+

A closure is a function that retains access to its outer scope's variables even after the outer function returns. Example: function counter() { let n = 0; return { inc: () => ++n, get: () => n }; } — the returned object 'closes over' n, keeping it alive and private. Uses: data privacy, factories, memoization, event handlers with state.

IExplain the event loop in detail.+

JS is single-threaded. The event loop: 1) Execute sync code on call stack. 2) When stack is empty, process ALL microtasks (Promise.then, queueMicrotask, MutationObserver). 3) Process ONE macrotask (setTimeout, setInterval, I/O, UI rendering). 4) Repeat. Key: microtasks always run before macrotasks. Nested microtasks run immediately too (can starve macrotasks).

IExplain 'this' — all rules with priority.+

Priority (highest to lowest): 1) new: this = new object. 2) bind/call/apply: this = specified object. 3) Object method: this = calling object. 4) Default: this = window (or undefined in strict mode). Arrow functions: no own this — always inherits from enclosing scope. This is why arrows are perfect for callbacks but wrong for object methods.

IWhat is the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)?+

The period between entering a block and the let/const declaration. Accessing the variable during TDZ throws ReferenceError. Exists because let/const ARE hoisted (the engine knows about them) but NOT initialized (they have no value yet). var doesn't have TDZ — it's hoisted and initialized as undefined.

IWhat is event delegation?+

Attach ONE listener to a parent instead of listeners on each child. Use e.target to determine which child was clicked. Benefits: works for dynamically added elements, fewer listeners, less memory. Pattern: parent.addEventListener('click', e => { if(e.target.matches('.item')) handleClick(e.target); }).

IWhat is Promise.all vs allSettled vs race vs any?+

all([p1,p2]): resolves when ALL resolve, rejects on FIRST rejection. allSettled: waits for ALL, returns [{status,value/reason}], never rejects. race: returns FIRST to settle (resolve or reject). any: returns FIRST to resolve, only rejects if ALL fail (AggregateError). Use all for parallel dependent tasks, allSettled for independent tasks, race for timeouts, any for fastest mirror.

IExplain prototypal inheritance.+

Every object has [[Prototype]] link to another object. Property lookup walks the chain: object → prototype → prototype's prototype → Object.prototype → null. Object.create(proto) creates object with custom prototype. ES6 classes are syntactic sugar — extends sets up the chain, super() calls parent constructor. instanceof checks the chain.

IShallow copy vs deep copy — methods and gotchas.+

Shallow: copies first level only. {...obj}, Object.assign({},obj), [...arr]. Nested objects share references. Deep: copies ALL levels. structuredClone(obj) (modern, handles dates/regex/maps/circular refs). JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj)) loses functions, undefined, dates, Infinity, NaN, circular refs. Always prefer structuredClone.

IWhat is the difference between call, apply, and bind?+

All set this explicitly. call(thisArg, a, b): invokes immediately, individual args. apply(thisArg, [a,b]): invokes immediately, array args. bind(thisArg, a): returns NEW function with bound this (doesn't invoke). Bind is for creating callbacks with specific context. Fun fact: Math.max.apply(null, arr) was the old way to spread — now use Math.max(...arr).

IWhat is a closure and how does it cause memory leaks?+

A closure retains references to outer variables. Memory leak: if a closure references a large object that's no longer needed, the GC can't collect it because the closure keeps it alive. Example: event listener closures that aren't removed. Fix: remove listeners on cleanup, null out references, use WeakRef for caches.

IExplain the classic for loop + var + setTimeout problem.+

for(var i=0;i<3;i++) setTimeout(()=>console.log(i),100) prints 3,3,3. Why: var is function-scoped — all callbacks share the SAME i, which is 3 after the loop finishes. Fixes: 1) Use let (block-scoped, new binding per iteration). 2) IIFE: ((j)=>setTimeout(()=>console.log(j),100))(i). 3) setTimeout(console.log, 100, i) (third arg).

IWhat are Map and Set? When use over Object/Array?+

Map: any key type, ordered, iterable, .size, no prototype pollution. Use when: keys are objects/functions, you need ordered iteration, frequent add/delete. Set: unique values only, .has() is O(1). Use for: deduplication [...new Set(arr)], membership testing. Object/Array are still better for JSON data and simple cases.

IWhat is debounce vs throttle?+

Debounce: waits until user STOPS doing something for N ms. Use: search input, resize handler. Throttle: executes at most once every N ms. Use: scroll handler, rate-limiting API calls. Debounce delays, throttle caps. Both use closures + setTimeout internally.

IWhat is the difference between Object.keys, values, entries?+

Object.keys(obj): array of own enumerable string keys. Object.values(obj): array of own enumerable values. Object.entries(obj): array of [key, value] pairs. None include Symbol keys or prototype properties. For Maps: use map.keys(), map.values(), map.entries().

IWhat is a generator function?+

function* gen() { yield 1; yield 2; } — can pause and resume. Returns an iterator with .next() → {value, done}. Use cases: lazy evaluation, infinite sequences, implementing custom iterables, controlled async flow. yield* delegates to another generator.

IExplain async iteration (for await...of).+

for await (const item of asyncIterable) {} — iterates over async iterables (streams, paginated APIs). The iterable's [Symbol.asyncIterator]() returns objects with async next() methods. Use with async generators: async function* fetchPages() { while(hasMore) { yield await fetchPage(); } }

IWhat is memoization?+

Caching function results based on inputs to avoid redundant computation. Implementation: function memoize(fn) { const cache = new Map(); return (...args) => { const key = JSON.stringify(args); if(cache.has(key)) return cache.get(key); const result = fn(...args); cache.set(key, result); return result; }; } Uses closures to persist the cache.

IWhat are tagged template literals?+

tag`text ${expr}` where tag is a function receiving strings array and interpolated values. Used by: styled-components (css`color: red`), GraphQL (gql`query{}`), i18n, SQL sanitization. The tag function can transform or validate the template. You can return anything, not just strings.

IWhat is the difference between Error types?+

Error: base class. TypeError: wrong type (null.prop). ReferenceError: undeclared variable. SyntaxError: parse error. RangeError: value out of range. URIError: bad URI. Custom: class AppError extends Error { constructor(msg,code) { super(msg); this.code=code; } }

IWhat is Symbol and when do you use it?+

Unique, immutable identifier: const id = Symbol('description'). Every Symbol() is unique. Not visible in for...in, Object.keys(), JSON.stringify(). Use cases: private-ish object keys, avoiding name collisions in libraries, well-known symbols (Symbol.iterator, Symbol.toPrimitive).

IExplain Object.freeze, seal, preventExtensions.+

Object.freeze(): no add, no delete, no modify — completely locked (shallow). Object.seal(): no add, no delete, CAN modify existing. Object.preventExtensions(): no add, CAN delete and modify. All are shallow — nested objects remain mutable. Object.isFrozen/isSealed/isExtensible to check.

IWhat is the arguments object?+

Array-like object available in regular functions containing all passed arguments. NOT available in arrow functions. NOT a real array (no map/filter). Convert: [...arguments] or Array.from(arguments). Modern replacement: rest parameters (...args) — always use rest instead.

IWhat is strict mode?+

Enabled with 'use strict'. Effects: prevents accidental globals (undeclared = error), disables with statement, makes eval safer, prevents duplicate params, this is undefined (not window) in regular functions, prohibits deleting non-configurable properties. Modules and classes are always strict.

IWhat is currying?+

Transforming f(a,b,c) into f(a)(b)(c). Each call returns a new function until all arguments are collected. Use: partial application const add5 = curry(add)(5), creating reusable utilities, point-free programming. Libraries like lodash/ramda provide curry helpers.

IWhat is the Proxy object?+

Intercepts operations on objects: get, set, delete, has, apply, construct, etc. new Proxy(target, handler). Real uses: validation (reject invalid values), reactive systems (Vue.js), logging/debugging, access control, default values for missing properties, negative array indexing.

IWhat is Reflect?+

Built-in object with methods matching Proxy trap names: Reflect.get(), Reflect.set(), Reflect.has(), etc. Provides the default behavior that Proxy traps can call. More reliable than direct operations — Reflect.defineProperty() returns boolean instead of throwing like Object.defineProperty().

IHow does garbage collection work in JavaScript?+

JS uses mark-and-sweep: the GC starts from roots (global, stack), marks all reachable objects, then sweeps (frees) unreachable ones. Common leak patterns: forgotten timers/intervals, detached DOM references in closures, growing arrays/maps never cleaned, circular references (handled by modern GC but still add pressure).

IWhat are WeakMap and WeakSet?+

WeakMap: keys must be objects, weakly held — GC can collect keys if no other references exist. Not iterable, no .size. Use: private data per object, caching without leaks. WeakSet: objects only, weakly held. Use: tracking visited objects, tagging objects without preventing GC.

IWhat is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous iteration?+

Sync: for...of with [Symbol.iterator], yields values immediately. Async: for await...of with [Symbol.asyncIterator], yields Promises. Async iterators are created with async function* generators. Use for: reading streams, paginated APIs, processing data as it arrives.

IWhat is the new keyword doing under the hood?+

1) Creates a new empty object. 2) Sets its [[Prototype]] to the constructor's .prototype. 3) Calls the constructor with this = the new object. 4) Returns the object (unless constructor explicitly returns an object). That's why arrow functions can't be constructors — they have no .prototype and can't bind this.

IWhat is structuredClone?+

Modern deep cloning: structuredClone(obj). Handles: nested objects, arrays, Maps, Sets, Dates, RegExp, ArrayBuffers, circular references. Does NOT handle: functions, DOM nodes, Error objects, property descriptors, prototype chain. Available in all modern browsers and Node.js 17+.

IExplain Promise chaining vs async/await.+

fetchUser().then(u => fetchPosts(u.id)).then(posts => render(posts)).catch(handleError) vs try { const u = await fetchUser(); const posts = await fetchPosts(u.id); render(posts); } catch(e) { handleError(e); } — async/await is cleaner for sequential operations. Promise.all + await is best for parallel operations.

IWhat is Object.defineProperty?+

Defines or modifies a property with fine-grained control: Object.defineProperty(obj, 'name', { value: 'Alice', writable: false, enumerable: true, configurable: false }). Also supports getter/setter: { get() { return this._name; }, set(v) { this._name = v; } }. Foundation for Vue 2's reactivity system.

IWhat are Iterables and Iterators?+

Iterable: object with [Symbol.iterator]() method (strings, arrays, Maps, Sets). Iterator: object with .next() → {value, done}. Custom: const iterable = { [Symbol.iterator]() { let i=0; return { next() { return i<3 ? {value:i++,done:false} : {done:true}; } }; } }. Enables for...of, spread, destructuring.

IWhat is the difference between setTimeout(fn,0) and queueMicrotask(fn)?+

setTimeout(fn,0): adds to macrotask queue — runs after ALL microtasks and any pending rendering. queueMicrotask(fn): adds to microtask queue — runs before any macrotasks, after current synchronous code. Promise.then callbacks are also microtasks. This order difference matters for timing-sensitive code.

IWhat is a Web Worker?+

Runs JS in a background thread — true parallelism. Created with new Worker('worker.js'). Communicate via postMessage/onmessage. Workers can't access DOM, window, or document. Use for: heavy computation, data processing, image manipulation. SharedWorker is shared across tabs. Service Worker handles network requests (offline, caching).

IWhat are ES2023-2024 new features?+

ES2023: Array.findLast/findLastIndex, toSorted/toReversed/toSpliced/with (immutable array methods), hashbang grammar. ES2024: Object.groupBy(), Map.groupBy(), Promise.withResolvers(), well-formed Unicode strings, Atomics.waitAsync. These are actively asked in senior interviews.

IWhat is the module system (import/export)?+

ES Modules: export const x = 1, export default fn, import {x} from './mod', import fn from './mod'. Live bindings (exports reflect current value). Static analysis (tree-shakable). import() for dynamic/lazy loading. CommonJS (require/module.exports) is Node.js legacy — synchronous, not tree-shakable.

IWhat is the difference between deep equality and shallow equality?+

Shallow: compares first-level values with ===. {a:1} !== {a:1} (different references). Deep: recursively compares all nested values. No built-in deep equality in JS. Options: JSON.stringify(a) === JSON.stringify(b) (order-dependent, loses types), lodash _.isEqual(), custom recursive function. React uses Object.is() for state comparison.

IWhat is short-circuit evaluation?+

&&: returns first falsy value (or last if all truthy). ||: returns first truthy value (or last if all falsy). Uses: user && user.name (safe access), name || 'Anonymous' (default). Gotcha: 0 || 'default' gives 'default' because 0 is falsy — use ?? for null/undefined only.

AImplement debounce from scratch.+

function debounce(fn, delay) { let timer; return (...args) => { clearTimeout(timer); timer = setTimeout(() => fn(...args), delay); }; } Uses closure to persist timer. Each call resets the timer. The function only fires after the user stops calling for the specified delay. Can be enhanced with leading/trailing options and cancel method.

AImplement throttle from scratch.+

function throttle(fn, limit) { let inThrottle; return (...args) => { if (!inThrottle) { fn(...args); inThrottle = true; setTimeout(() => inThrottle = false, limit); } }; } Ensures function runs at most once per interval. First call executes immediately. Uses closure for the flag. Can be enhanced with trailing call option.

AImplement a simple Promise from scratch.+

Core: store state (pending/fulfilled/rejected), value, and callback queue. resolve() transitions state and runs stored callbacks. .then() returns a new Promise (for chaining), stores callbacks if pending, runs immediately if already resolved. Key: callbacks must be async (microtask) per spec. This demonstrates understanding of the Promise specification (Promises/A+).

AImplement curry function.+

const curry = (fn) => { const arity = fn.length; return function curried(...args) { return args.length >= arity ? fn(...args) : (...more) => curried(...args, ...more); }; } Recursively collects arguments until enough are gathered. curry(add)(1)(2) and curry(add)(1,2) both work. Placeholder support adds complexity.

AImplement Function.prototype.bind polyfill.+

Function.prototype.myBind = function(context, ...boundArgs) { const fn = this; return function(...args) { return fn.apply(context, [...boundArgs, ...args]); }; } Uses closure to capture context and partial args. Real bind also handles 'new' calls and preserves prototype chain — this simplified version covers 90% of interview expectations.

AExplain the JavaScript engine pipeline.+

1) Parsing: source → AST (Abstract Syntax Tree). 2) Interpretation: AST → bytecode (executed immediately by interpreter). 3) Profiling: engine monitors 'hot' functions (called frequently). 4) JIT Compilation: hot functions compiled to optimized machine code. 5) Deoptimization: if assumptions are wrong (e.g., type changed), falls back to interpreter. V8 uses Ignition (interpreter) + TurboFan (compiler).

AWhat are memory leaks in JavaScript? How to detect and fix?+

Common leaks: 1) Forgotten timers/intervals (clear them!). 2) Detached DOM references in closures. 3) Closures retaining large objects unnecessarily. 4) Growing caches without eviction. 5) Event listeners not removed. Detect: Chrome DevTools Memory tab — take heap snapshots, compare, find growing objects. Fix: cleanup functions, WeakMap/WeakRef for caches, remove listeners, null out references.

AExplain the Proxy/Reflect API with real use cases.+

Proxy intercepts 13 operations: get, set, has, deleteProperty, apply, construct, etc. Reflect provides default behavior for each trap. Use cases: 1) Validation: reject invalid values in set trap. 2) Reactive systems: trigger UI updates on property change (Vue 3's reactivity). 3) Negative indexing: arr[-1]. 4) Auto-vivification: auto-create nested objects on access. 5) Access logging: track all property reads/writes.

AExplain JavaScript module systems (ESM vs CJS vs AMD vs UMD).+

ESM: import/export, static analysis, tree-shakable, async loading, live bindings. CJS: require/module.exports, Node.js legacy, synchronous, copies values. AMD: define([],(deps)=>{}), browser async loading (RequireJS), mostly obsolete. UMD: wrapper that works in all systems (CJS/AMD/global). Modern: use ESM everywhere. Node.js supports both via .mjs extension or package.json "type":"module".

AWhat is tail call optimization (TCO)?+

When the last action of a function is a function call, the engine can reuse the current stack frame instead of adding a new one — prevents stack overflow for recursive functions. Only Safari implements TCO in strict mode. Other engines don't. Workaround: convert recursion to iteration, or use trampolining: function trampoline(fn) { let result = fn(); while(typeof result === 'function') result = result(); return result; }

AImplement EventEmitter from scratch.+

class EventEmitter { constructor() { this.events = {}; } on(event, fn) { (this.events[event] ??= []).push(fn); return this; } off(event, fn) { this.events[event] = this.events[event]?.filter(f => f !== fn); } emit(event, ...args) { this.events[event]?.forEach(fn => fn(...args)); } once(event, fn) { const wrapper = (...args) => { fn(...args); this.off(event, wrapper); }; this.on(event, wrapper); } } Demonstrates closures, this binding, and the observer pattern.

AWhat is the difference between concurrency and parallelism in JS?+

Concurrency: multiple tasks make progress (event loop — one thread, interleaved execution). Parallelism: multiple tasks run simultaneously (Web Workers — multiple threads). JS main thread is concurrent, not parallel. Web Workers enable true parallelism. SharedArrayBuffer + Atomics enable shared memory between workers (with synchronization).

AExplain WeakRef and FinalizationRegistry.+

WeakRef: holds weak reference to object. ref.deref() returns object or undefined if GC'd. Use for caches that shouldn't prevent garbage collection. FinalizationRegistry: register callbacks that fire when objects are GC'd. const registry = new FinalizationRegistry(heldValue => { cleanup(heldValue); }); registry.register(obj, 'cleanup-token'). Both are non-deterministic — GC timing is unpredictable.

AImplement deep clone without structuredClone.+

function deepClone(obj, seen = new WeakMap()) { if (obj === null || typeof obj !== 'object') return obj; if (seen.has(obj)) return seen.get(obj); if (obj instanceof Date) return new Date(obj); if (obj instanceof RegExp) return new RegExp(obj); const clone = Array.isArray(obj) ? [] : {}; seen.set(obj, clone); for (const key of Reflect.ownKeys(obj)) { clone[key] = deepClone(obj[key], seen); } return clone; } Handles circular refs with WeakMap, preserves Date/RegExp, uses Reflect.ownKeys for symbols.

AWhat is the Temporal API (Stage 3)?+

Modern date/time API replacing the broken Date object. Temporal.Now.instant(), Temporal.PlainDate.from('2025-01-15'), Temporal.ZonedDateTime. Features: immutable, timezone-aware, calendar support, duration arithmetic, no month-indexing issues (January is 1 not 0). Still Stage 3 but polyfillable. Will eventually obsolete moment.js and date-fns for most use cases.

AImplement pipe and compose functions.+

const pipe = (...fns) => (x) => fns.reduce((v, fn) => fn(v), x); Left-to-right composition. const compose = (...fns) => (x) => fns.reduceRight((v, fn) => fn(v), x); Right-to-left. Usage: const transform = pipe(trim, lowercase, capitalize); transform(' HELLO '). Fundamental functional programming pattern. TC39 has a pipeline operator proposal (x |> fn).

AExplain the Iterator and Iterable protocols in depth.+

Iterable: has [Symbol.iterator]() returning an iterator. Iterator: has .next() returning {value, done}. Custom: make any object iterable. Async versions use [Symbol.asyncIterator]() and async next(). Generators auto-implement both protocols. Consumed by: for...of, spread, destructuring, Array.from(), Promise.all(), yield*.

AWhat is the structured clone algorithm?+

The algorithm behind structuredClone(), postMessage(), IndexedDB, and history API. Handles: objects, arrays, Maps, Sets, Dates, RegExp, ArrayBuffers, Blobs, Files, ImageData, circular references. Does NOT handle: functions, DOM nodes, property descriptors, getters/setters, prototype chain, Error objects (in some engines). It's what makes Web Worker communication possible.

AExplain AbortController and AbortSignal.+

const controller = new AbortController(); fetch(url, {signal: controller.signal}).catch(e => { if(e.name === 'AbortError') console.log('Cancelled'); }); controller.abort(); Cancels fetch requests, event listeners, and any async operation that accepts a signal. Multiple operations can share one signal. Use in React cleanup: useEffect(() => { const ctrl = new AbortController(); fetch(url, {signal: ctrl.signal}); return () => ctrl.abort(); }, [url])

AWhat are SharedArrayBuffer and Atomics?+

SharedArrayBuffer: memory shared between main thread and workers (unlike regular ArrayBuffer which is transferred/copied). Atomics: provides thread-safe operations on shared memory — Atomics.add(), Atomics.load(), Atomics.store(), Atomics.wait()/notify() for synchronization. Required for high-performance parallel computing in JS. Requires cross-origin isolation headers (COOP/COEP) due to Spectre mitigations.
